verses.text: “Do not judge, so that you will not be judged.
For with the measure you judge, it will be measured to you in return.
Why do you see the speck in your brother's eye, but miss the log in your own?
How can you say to your sister, 'Let me remove the speck from your eye,' while a log is still in yours?
Hypocrite! First remove the log from your own eye, then you will clearly remove the speck from your brothers' or sisters' eye.
Do not give what is holy to dogs, or throw your pearls before pigs, lest they trample them underfoot and turn on you and tear you apart.”
“Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and it will be opened to you.
For anyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it will be opened.
Who among you, if your son asks for bread, would give him a stone?
Or if your daughter asks for a fish, would give her a serpent?
If then you being evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to the ones who ask Him?”
“Therefore, in all things, whatever you want people to do for you, do the same for them. This is core in the Law and the Prophets.”
“Enter through the narrow gate, for the gate is wide and the road is broad that leads astray to destruction, and many are the ones who enter through it.
For the gate is narrow and the road is constrained that leads to life, and few are the ones who find it.
Be careful of false prophets, who come to you in garments of sheep but inwardly are rapacious wolves.
By their fruits you will know them. Are grapes gathered from thorns, or figs from thistles?
In the same way, every good tree bears good fruit, but a rotten tree bears bad fruit.
A good tree cannot bear bad fruit, and a rotten tree cannot bear good fruit.
Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire.
Therefore indeed by their fruit you will know them.
“Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter into the kingdom of heaven, but the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven.
On that day many will say to me, ‘Lord, Lord’, did we not prophesy in your name, cast out demons in your name, and perform many miracles in your name?'
Then I will declare to them, ‘I never knew you. Depart from me, you workers of lawlessness.’”
“Therefore, everyone who hears these words of mine and does them is like a wise person who built their house on the rock.
The rain fell, the rivers rose, the winds blew and beat against that house, yet it did not fall, for it had been founded on the rock.
But everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them is like a foolish person who built their house on the sand.
The rain fell, the rivers rose, the winds blew and beat against that house, and it fell, and mighty was its fall.”
When Jesus finished his words, the crowds were astonished at his teaching.
For he was teaching as one with authority and not like their scribes.

footnotes.content: 'Dearest or dear one''s intentionally shifts from brother/sister to avoid over-literality while emphasizing relational accountability in the faith community.'

The phrase “turn on you and tear you to pieces” comes from the Greek verbs straphentes (“to turn”) and rhēxōsin (“to tear apart”), painting a scene of sudden aggression. After rejecting what is holy, the pigs lash out at the giver. Jesus warns that offering sacred truth to those hostile or unreceptive may provoke backlash. The imagery of dogs and pigs—symbols of impurity in Jewish culture—underscores the need for discernment, not elitism, in sharing what is holy.

The phrase “is foundational in the Law and the Prophets” reflects the Greek wording houtos gar estin ho nomos kai hoi prophētai, which literally means, “for this is the Law and the Prophets.” While some translations render this as “sums up,” such phrasing could mislead—since in Matthew 22:40, Jesus names love of God and neighbor as the two great commandments upon which all the Law and the Prophets depend. The Golden Rule captures the ethical essence of how we treat others, but not the full relational scope of Jesus' teaching. The phrase “foundational in” was chosen because it affirms the deep significance of this principle within the Hebrew Scriptures, while avoiding the implication that it alone captures the entirety of the Law's purpose. It reflects a core moral pillar without overstating its exclusivity.

Jesus' words here express more than denial—they expose a relational void. “I never knew you” does not mean God lacked information about them, but that there was no intimacy, no communion of spirit. These were people who claimed His name but lived in contradiction to His will. To be called a “worker of lawlessness” is not just about sinful acts—it means living in persistent rebellion, outside the order and heart of God's kingdom. This is not rejection of sincere strugglers, but of those who practiced spiritual performance without transformation. The command to “depart” is the sorrowful justice of a Savior whose invitation was ignored until it was too late.

This verse portrays the end of a life built without obedience to Jesus' teaching. The fall of the house represents the spiritual ruin that comes from hearing His word but not living by it. The contrast throughout the parable makes clear: only those who build on the foundation of His word—by doing it—will withstand the storms of judgment and life.

The word exousia refers to intrinsic authority—not merely permission or delegated power, but divine right. Jesus' authority sharply contrasted with that of the scribes, who taught by citing rabbinic tradition. While they relied on precedent, He spoke with the weight of original, divine truth—as one who wrote the Law, not merely interpreted it. The crowd's astonishment was not merely intellectual but spiritual. They sensed the difference between inherited commentary and living revelation. Even His enemies later confessed, “No one ever spoke like this man” (John 7:46).
